name: Risk Factor Linguistic Drift as Forward Return Signal
mechanism: When companies revise risk factor disclosures, the language change direction (adding vs removing risks, making risks more vs less specific) signals management's true risk assessment. Companies that quietly remove or water down previously disclosed risks are signaling improvement; companies that add new, specific risks are signaling deterioration. The LLM advantage: systematic measurement of risk factor text changes (additions, removals, specificity shifts) across thousands of 10-K filings.
llm_advantage: Deterministic linguistic feature extraction across risk factor sections. Risk factor text is formulaic enough that hedge/certainty density changes are meaningful signals.
why_underweighted: Risk factor language is heavily lawyered. Changes may be driven by legal compliance rather than genuine risk assessment changes. Also, risk factors are backward-looking in practice.

known_biases: Risk factor sections in 10-Q may be abbreviated or refer to 10-K
Section boundary detection may miss non-standard formatting
Risk factor changes may be driven by legal/regulatory requirements, not business conditions
Survivorship bias for stocks delisted before ~2017

falsifiable_prediction: Companies in the top quartile of risk factor hedge density increase should underperform bottom quartile by at least 300bps annualized over the subsequent 3 months.
biggest_weakness: Risk factor language is heavily influenced by legal counsel. Changes may be compliance-driven rather than information-driven. Also, quarterly changes in risk factors tend to be minimal — annual 10-K comparisons are more meaningful.
